text: The video shows a protruding belly button, visually consistent with an umbilical hernia.
The camera view from inside the abdomen reveals a visible defect or hole in the abdominal wall.
The surgeon displays the abdominal wall, which is covered by the thin, glistening layer of peritoneum.
The surgeon pulls the peritoneal layer down and away from the fascia, the strongest layer of the abdominal wall, revealing the separation between these anatomical structures.
The surgeon exposes the peritoneum, revealing a yellow layer of adipose tissue covering this area of the abdominal wall.
The surgeon begins removing the peritoneum from the fascia, preparing to situate the mesh directly onto the strong fascia layer of the abdominal wall.
The surgeon encounters the hernia, with a significant amount of yellow adipose tissue protruding through the defect in the abdominal wall.
The surgeon completes the removal of tissue from the hernia space, revealing the fascia which is now skeletonized and free of surrounding tissue.
The surgeon visually identifies the true edges of the hernia defect, allowing for an estimation of the hernia's size.
The surgeon uses a left-handed instrument to measure the hernia defect, which is approximately three centimeters in diameter.
The surgeon selects an 11.4 cm circular mesh to cover the hernia defect, preparing to place it over the exposed area where yellow tissue was previously observed.

The surgeon continues to separate the peritoneum from the fascia, creating a pocket large enough to accommodate the eleven point four centimeter circle-shaped mesh, extending beyond the visible hernia defect.
The surgeon exposes the fascia sufficiently for mesh placement and proceeds to close the hernia defect using sutures.
The surgeon tightens the sutures under tension to approximate the edges of the hernia defect, aiming to close the gap between the separated tissue.
The surgeon places an 11.4 cm circle-shaped mesh flat against the abdominal wall, ensuring the largest exposed surface area to offload tension from the sutures closing the hernia defect.
The surgeon separates the flimsy peritoneum from the stronger fascia, preparing the surgical field for the mesh to bind directly to the fascia, ensuring a robust repair.
The surgeon sutures the mesh to the abdominal wall using an absorbable stitch, securing it in place over the hernia site.
The surgeon closes the pocket in the peritoneum, which was created to conceal the mesh from the intestines in the abdomen.
The video shows three separate eight millimeter incisions on the left side of the abdomen, with no incisions at the belly button.
